preFix.title: Nuno-felted scarf on cotton gauze
preFix.subtitle: A 180 × 30 cm scarf worked on lightweight cotton gauze with a merino roving overlay
preFix.excerpt: Nuno felting bonds wool fibres through a fabric substrate. This scarf uses lightweight cotton gauze as the base, with merino roving laid in diagonal stripes on one side. As the wool shrinks during fulling, the cotton gathers into a permanent crinkled texture. Pre-felt dimensions: 360 × 60 cm, allowing 50% length and 40% width shrinkage.
preFix.sourceNotes: Nuno felting on cotton gauze is a well-established technique in contemporary fibre arts, developed by Polly Stirling in the 1990s and widely taught in the UK and internationally. Technique synthesised from general nuno felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: Nuno felting
bonds wool into a fabric
substrate
. The wool fibres migrate through the weave during
fulling

and lock in place permanently. As the wool shrinks, it pulls the gauze with it, creating a gathered, crinkled texture that is lighter and draper than a fully felted wool scarf. This scarf starts at 360 × 60 cm (pre-felt dimensions) and finishes at 180 × 30 cm. The shrinkage is intentional: 50% on length and 40% on width. Sparse diagonal stripes of
roving
are used rather than a full coverage, which keeps the scarf lightweight and makes the gathered gauze pattern more pronounced between the wool areas.

items.name: Cotton gauze or muslin, open weave, pre-washed and dried
Merino roving, 19-21 micron, in one or two colours

content.text: Method
Step 1: Pre-wash the gauze

Machine-wash the cotton gauze at 40°C and tumble dry or iron flat. Pre-washing removes sizing and causes the initial cotton shrinkage, so the gauze will not shrink further during felting. If you skip this step, the gauze may shrink unevenly and distort the finished scarf.
Step 2: Lay out the gauze and mark stripe positions

Lay the gauze flat on the bamboo rolling mat. Lightly mark diagonal stripe positions with a chalk pencil or pins every 25-30 cm along the length. The stripes should run at approximately 45 degrees to the length of the scarf. Each stripe will be a loose band of roving about 5-6 cm wide.
Step 3: Lay the roving stripes

Pull thin wisps of roving and lay them diagonally across the gauze following the marked stripe positions. Keep the roving sparse: one or two thin wisps per stripe, not a solid mat. The goal is a light overlay, not full coverage. Sparse laying allows the wool to migrate through the gauze weave more easily and produces a more pronounced gathered texture.
Step 4: Wet down through the felting net

Lay the felting net over the full surface. Drizzle warm soapy water (40°C) over the net until both the roving and the gauze are fully saturated. Press down firmly through the net with flat hands. The water needs to penetrate the gauze as well as the wool.
Step 5: Rub through the net

Rub with flat hands through the net in small circular movements for 12-15 minutes, working across the full length of the scarf. The roving must begin to migrate through the gauze; you should see the fibres starting to poke through the back of the gauze when you lift a corner to check. This is the sign that
pre-felt
stage is approaching.
Step 6: Roll in the bamboo mat

Remove the net and roll the scarf inside the bamboo mat. Roll back and forth firmly for 10 minutes. Unroll, turn the scarf 90 degrees, and roll for another 10 minutes. The gauze will begin to gather and crinkle as you do this. Continue rolling in all four orientations until the scarf measures approximately 220 × 40 cm (it will continue to shrink during the throwing stage).
Step 7: Full by throwing

Gather the scarf into a loose bundle and throw it firmly onto the felting mat 30 times. Reposition between throws. The gather and crinkle will deepen significantly during this stage. Measure after every 10 throws. Stop when the scarf reaches 180 × 30 cm, or as close as possible. Do not full past the intended dimensions.
Step 8: Rinse and dry

Rinse thoroughly in water at the same temperature as the wash water (30-40°C). Squeeze gently. Hang to dry, pulling lightly along the length while still damp to encourage a uniform drape. The crinkled texture is permanent once dry.

items.fix: Check that the gauze is an open-weave fabric (muslin or cheesecloth weight). If the weave is correct, increase rubbing time to 20 minutes through the net before rolling.
items.cause: The gauze weave is too tight for the wool fibres to migrate through, or rubbing was not sustained long enough
items.problem: The roving is lifting off the gauze rather than bonding through it

items.fix: Measure every 10 minutes during fulling and stop when dimensions are reached. Overfulling is irreversible.
items.cause: Fulling was continued past the intended shrinkage point
items.problem: The scarf has shrunk to less than 160 cm

items.fix: Ensure roving stripes are evenly spaced and of similar weight. Roll in all four orientations to distribute shrinkage evenly.
items.cause: The roving was spread unevenly, or rubbing pressure was not consistent across the length.
items.problem: The crinkle is patchy: some areas are flat, others deeply gathered

items.fix: Nuno felting requires patient light rubbing in the early stages. Always rub through the net for the first 10 minutes. Increase pressure only once you can see fibres migrating through the back of the gauze.
items.cause: Rubbing pressure was too heavy before the fibres had begun to bond
items.problem: The gauze has torn or developed holes

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a wool-safe detergent; the cotton gauze component tolerates gentle machine washing on a wool or delicate cycle. Lay flat or hang to dry. The crinkled texture returns when dry after each wash. Do not tumble dry: heat will cause further shrinkage and may distort the drape.
